back to home

Research

My research has focused on adapting deep learning models to the complexity of biological data through novel architectures, regularizations, and training setups. I'm especially interested in representation learning and reasoning models.

For a full publication list, see my Google Scholar profile.

Selected Projects

Newest work appears first.

2025 · CellSpliceNet for alternative splicing across neurons This project develops interpretable multimodal models for studying alternative splicing in C. elegans neurons. The goal is to combine multiple biological signals into a clearer view of neuron-specific splicing behavior.
bioRxiv preprint | NeurIPS workshop paper
2024-2025 · ProtSCAPE ProtSCAPE studies how learned representations can organize protein conformations from molecular dynamics simulations. It is aimed at making structural ensembles easier to map, compare, and reason about.
arXiv preprint | conference presentation
2023 · Learning representations for biomolecular analysis, optimization, and design My dissertation brings together several themes from my work on representation learning, generative modeling, and biomolecular optimization. It focuses on how learned biological representations can support both scientific understanding and design tasks.
dissertation
2022-2023 · Regularized latent spaces for protein optimization This line of work explores transformer models that learn smooth protein latent spaces for both generation and optimization. The central idea is that good representations can make sequence search more efficient and make protein design more controllable.
Nature Machine Intelligence paper | ReLSO preprint
2022 · Molecular graph generation via geometric scattering This project combines graph signal processing ideas with generative modeling for molecules. It investigates whether geometric scattering representations can capture chemically meaningful structure for generation tasks.
conference paper
2020-2021 · Predicting transfusion need in acute GI bleeding This clinical machine learning project uses neural networks to predict whether ICU patients with acute gastrointestinal bleeding will require red blood cell transfusion. The work is aimed at earlier risk stratification and decision support in high-acuity care settings.
Scientific Reports paper | conference abstract
2020 · RNA folding landscapes with deep graph embeddings This project investigates whether deep graph embeddings can recover meaningful structure in RNA folding landscapes. It connects RNA secondary structure analysis with representation learning over graph-based molecular objects.
IEEE Big Data paper | arXiv preprint
2017 · Molecular mechanisms in cancer biology This earlier work examined how vitamin K epoxide reductase regulates androgen receptor activity. It contributed to understanding molecular mechanisms relevant to cancer biology and translational research.
journal paper
